1.synthesis: Amid the death-decree, a Levite couple bears a son, and seeing that “he was a fine child,” his mother hides him three months, then sets him in a waterproofed basket among the reeds of the Nile (vv.2–3). Pharaoh’s own daughter finds him, has compassion, and — by the watching sister’s clever arrangement — pays the child’s own mother to nurse him (vv.6–9). She names him Moses, “Because I drew him out of the water” (v.10). The integrative thread is the hidden hand of providence: the very river decreed for the deaths of Hebrew sons becomes the cradle of their savior, and the deliverer is reared, at Pharaoh’s expense, in Pharaoh’s own house.
1.historical_context: The basket (Hebrew tebah) is the same rare word used for Noah’s ark (Genesis 6:14), the only two vessels of salvation in the Pentateuch — a deliberate link binding Moses’ rescue to the great deliverance through water. Egyptian princesses did adopt children, and being “drawn out” of the Nile gave Moses a name resonant in both Hebrew (mashah, to draw out) and Egyptian (a common name-element meaning “son”). His Levite parents, later named Amram and Jochebed (Exodus 6:20), acted “by faith,” unafraid of the king’s edict (Hebrews 11:23).
1.literary_structure: The narrative is rich with irony and reversal: Pharaoh commanded death by the Nile (Exodus 1:22), and from the Nile comes life; the destroyer’s daughter becomes the preserver; the mother condemned to lose her son is paid to keep him. The watching sister (v.4) and the ark in the reeds heighten the suspense. The naming (v.10) seals the theme of being “drawn out,” which Moses will one day enact for the whole nation drawn out of Egypt through the sea.
1.theological_application: God’s providence works through ordinary courage and maternal love to overrule the decrees of tyrants (Hebrews 11:23); the faith that defies an ungodly edict and entrusts a child to God is honored. The passage assures believers that God prepares his deliverers long before the hour of deliverance, often in hidden and unlikely ways, weaving even an enemy’s household into his saving plan (Proverbs 21:1). Nothing — not a death-decree, not a river — can thwart the purposes of God.
1.christology: Moses, the appointed deliverer hidden from a murderous king and preserved through water, foreshadows Christ, hidden from Herod and brought up out of Egypt (Matthew 2:15). The ark of bulrushes, like Noah’s ark, prefigures salvation through the waters that elsewhere bring death — fulfilled in the baptism into Christ through which we pass from death to life (1 Peter 3:20–21). The one drawn out of the water to draw out a people points to the greater Redeemer who leads the new exodus (Luke 9:31).

11.synthesis: Grown to manhood, Moses goes out to his people, sees an Egyptian beating a Hebrew, and — “looking this way and that” — kills the Egyptian and hides him in the sand (vv.11–12). The next day, rebuked by a quarreling Hebrew (“Who made you a prince and a judge over us?”), he realizes the deed is known (v.14). When Pharaoh seeks to kill him, Moses flees to Midian (v.15). The integrative thread is the premature and self-reliant attempt at deliverance: Moses rightly identifies with his oppressed people and rightly burns at injustice, but acts in his own strength and timing, and must spend forty years in exile before God will send him in God’s strength.
11.historical_context: Stephen’s sermon reveals Moses’ inner reasoning: “he supposed that his brothers would understand that God was giving them salvation by his hand, but they did not understand” (Acts 7:25). Moses, “instructed in all the wisdom of the Egyptians” (Acts 7:22) and by faith “refusing to be called the son of Pharaoh’s daughter” (Hebrews 11:24), chose solidarity with the afflicted people of God. Midian, in the desert east of Egypt, becomes his place of refuge and, providentially, of preparation.
11.literary_structure: The furtive glance “this way and that” (v.12) and the burial in the sand mark the act as something Moses knows to be compromised. The taunt “Who made you a prince and a judge?” (v.14) ironically anticipates the office God will indeed give him, and will be hurled at him again by Israel in the wilderness (Acts 7:35). The flight to Midian (v.15) parallels Jacob’s earlier flight, the chosen one driven to a far country before his return.
11.theological_application: Zeal for justice, however genuine, must be submitted to God’s way and timing; Moses’ self-appointed deliverance fails and drives him into exile (Proverbs 19:2). God often takes his servants through long seasons of obscurity and humbling before entrusting them with their calling — the impulsive prince must become the meek shepherd (Numbers 12:3). The believer learns that the flesh’s attempts to advance God’s purposes by force are not the same as the Spirit’s deliverance, and that waiting on God is not wasted time.
11.christology: The rejected deliverer — “Who made you a ruler and a judge over us?” (v.14) — prefigures Christ, the true Ruler and Judge rejected by his own (Luke 19:14; John 1:11), as Stephen explicitly draws the parallel (Acts 7:35). Where Moses delivered prematurely and by the sword, Christ would deliver at the appointed time and by laying down his own life. Moses’ identification with his suffering people, esteeming their reproach, foreshadows the Son who shared our affliction to redeem us (Hebrews 11:26).

16.synthesis: At a Midianite well Moses defends the seven daughters of the priest of Midian from hostile shepherds and waters their flock (vv.16–17). He is welcomed into the household, marries Zipporah, and names his son Gershom, “for he said, ‘I have been a sojourner in a foreign land’” (v.22). Meanwhile the king of Egypt dies, and “the people of Israel groaned because of their slavery and cried out… And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob. God saw the people of Israel — and God knew” (vv.24–25). The integrative thread is the turning point of the whole book: as Moses settles into obscurity, God’s covenant memory stirs, and the four divine verbs — heard, remembered, saw, knew — announce that deliverance is at hand.
16.historical_context: The well scene (vv.16–17) is a classic betrothal type-scene, like those of Isaac and Jacob (Genesis 24; Genesis 29), marking Moses as a patriarch in the chosen line. The priest of Midian (Reuel/Jethro) becomes Moses’ father-in-law and counselor (Exodus 18). Gershom’s name (v.22) captures Moses’ alienation, while the forty years of shepherding in the desert (Acts 7:30) prepare the man who will shepherd Israel through that same wilderness.
16.literary_structure: The chapter ends with a deliberate crescendo of four verbs with God as subject — heard, remembered, saw, knew (vv.24–25) — a literary hinge that shifts the narrative from human helplessness to divine initiative, setting up the burning bush. “Remembered his covenant” (v.24) is the theological key: the Exodus is grounded not in Israel’s merit but in God’s sworn promise to the patriarchs (Genesis 15:13–14).
16.theological_application: God hears the groaning of his afflicted people and acts according to his covenant, not their worthiness (v.24; Psalm 105:8). The believer is assured that no cry of the oppressed escapes God’s notice and that his apparent delay is covenant patience, not indifference. The four verbs teach that God’s “remembering” is never mere recollection but the prelude to mighty action (Luke 1:54–55); when God “knows” his people’s suffering, redemption is near.
16.christology: God’s remembering of his covenant and seeing of his people’s misery culminates not only in the Exodus but in the incarnation, when, to fulfill the promise to Abraham, he “visited and redeemed his people” (Luke 1:68, 72). Moses the shepherd-in-exile, prepared in the wilderness to lead God’s flock, prefigures Christ the Good Shepherd (John 10:11). The God who “knew” Israel’s affliction draws near in the Son who is “Immanuel,” entering our bondage to lead the great deliverance (Hebrews 2:14–15).
